title: Scottish drop scones
subtitle: Small, thick pancakes cooked on a griddle or hot pan
excerpt: Thick, small pancakes made from a baking powder batter, cooked in a dry pan until bubbles appear on the surface and the edges look set, then flipped once. Also called Scotch pancakes. Eaten warm with butter and jam or honey. A Scottish breakfast and teatime staple.
sourceNotes: Drop scones or Scotch pancakes are a Scottish breakfast and tea bread, distinct from English pancakes (which are thin and crepe-like) and American pancakes (which are thicker but also larger). The Scottish version is small, about 8 cm across, and cooked in a heavy pan without oil. They are called 'drop scones' because the batter is dropped from a spoon.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in stacks with baking paper between. Reheat in a toaster or warm pan.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe — they are as easy to make in bulk and freeze well.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Rest the batter for 10 minutes before cooking.

content.text: The batter should be thick enough to hold its shape when dropped from a spoon, not run. If it spreads too thin, the scones will be flat. A correctly thick batter produces a scone with a slight dome on top.

Cook in a dry, heavy pan — no oil or butter. The heat should be medium: too high and the outside burns before the centre sets; too low and the scones do not colour. The signal to flip is when bubbles appear all over the surface and the edges look matte rather than wet.

items.prepNote: approximately — add to the right consistency

content.text: Combine the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{granulated-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Make a well in the centre. Add the {{eggs}} and half the {{whole-milk}}. Whisk from the centre outwards until a thick, smooth batter forms. Add more milk until the batter falls thickly from the spoon but does not pour. Rest for 10 minutes. Heat a heavy frying pan or griddle over medium heat until a drop of water sizzles and evaporates. Drop heaped tablespoons of batter onto the dry pan, spacing them 3 cm apart. Cook for 2 to 3 minutes until bubbles appear all over the surface and the edges look set. Flip and cook for 1 to 2 minutes more until the second side is golden. Serve warm with butter and jam.

items.symptom: Dark on the outside, doughy and raw in the centre
items.fix: Reduce to medium heat. The scones need 2 to 3 minutes per side — too hot and the outside colours before the centre cooks

items.symptom: Pale and flat, no dome or colour
items.cause: Batter too thin, or heat too low
items.fix: If the batter pours rather than drops, add a tablespoon more flour. Ensure the pan is properly preheated before adding the first batch

content.text: Scottish drop scones are a weekend breakfast and afternoon tea food. They appear at Highland Games, school fairs, and Sunday morning tables throughout Scotland and northern England. They are at their best immediately off the pan, served with good butter melting over the warm surface.
